summary:
The Ethiopian Solomonic Dynasty (restored 1270 AD) was established when Yekuno Amlak overthrew the last [[event:2215|Zagwe Dynasty]] king, claiming descent from King Solomon of Israel through the biblical Queen of Sheba — a legendary lineage that gave the dynasty both religious legitimacy and a claim to represent the continuation of ancient Israelite kingship in Africa. The Solomonic dynasty ruled Ethiopia, with interruptions, from 1270 until Emperor Haile Selassie's deposition in 1974 — one of the longest-lived royal dynasties in world history, spanning over 700 years.

The Solomonic restoration coincided with the compilation of the Kebra Nagast ('Glory of Kings'), the great Ethiopian national epic that narrates how the Queen of Sheba (called Makeda in Ethiopian tradition) visited Solomon, bore his son Menelik, and how Menelik brought the Ark of the Covenant to Ethiopia — where, according to Ethiopian Orthodox tradition, it still resides in the Church of Our Lady Mary of Zion in Aksum. The Kebra Nagast provided the theological foundation for the Solomonic claim and shaped Ethiopian royal ideology for centuries. The dynasty presided over the expansion of the Ethiopian state through the highlands, the consolidation of the Ethiopian Orthodox Church as the national institution, and the famous contacts with European Crusaders who sought the legendary 'Prester John' — a Christian king beyond the Islamic world — in Ethiopia. Emperor Zara Yaqob (reigned 1434–1468) presided over a period of ecclesiastical consolidation and theological controversy that produced important Ethiopian Christian literature.
